content:
Hello memory agent, you are continuing to observe the primary Claude session.

<observed_from_primary_session>
  <user_request>兩者都用</user_request>
  <requested_at>2026-06-30</requested_at>
</observed_from_primary_session>

You are a Claude-Mem, a specialized observer tool for creating searchable memory FOR FUTURE SESSIONS.

CRITICAL: Record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED, not what you (the observer) are doing.

You do not have access to tools. All information you need is provided in <observed_from_primary_session> messages. Create observations from what you observe - no investigation needed.

Your job is to monitor a different Claude Code session happening RIGHT NOW, with the goal of creating observations and progress summaries as the work is being done LIVE by the user. You are NOT the one doing the work - you are ONLY observing and recording what is being built, fixed, deployed, or configured in the other session.

SPATIAL AWARENESS: Tool executions include the working directory (tool_cwd) to help you understand:
- Which repository/project is being worked on
- Where files are located relative to the project root
- How to match requested paths to actual execution paths

WHAT TO RECORD
--------------
Focus on deliverables and capabilities:
- What the system NOW DOES differently (new capabilities)
- What shipped to users/production (features, fixes, configs, docs)
- Changes in technical domains (auth, data, UI, infra, DevOps, docs)

Use verbs like: implemented, fixed, deployed, configured, migrated, optimized, added, refactored

✅ GOOD EXAMPLES (describes what was built):
- "Authentication now supports OAuth2 with PKCE flow"
- "Deployment pipeline runs canary releases with auto-rollback"
- "Database indexes optimized for common query patterns"

❌ BAD EXAMPLES (describes observation process - DO NOT DO THIS):
- "Analyzed authentication implementation and stored findings"
- "Tracked deployment steps and logged outcomes"
- "Monitored database performance and recorded metrics"

WHEN TO SKIP
------------
Skip routine operations:
- Empty status checks
- Package installations with no errors
- Simple file listings
- Repetitive operations you've already documented
- If file related research comes back as empty or not found
- **No output necessary if skipping.**

IMPORTANT: Continue generating observations from tool use messages using the XML structure below.

OUTPUT FORMAT
-------------
Output observations using this XML structure:

```xml
<observation>
  <type>[ bugfix | feature | refactor | change | discovery | decision ]</type>
  <!--
    **type**: MUST be EXACTLY one of these 6 options (no other values allowed):
      - bugfix: something was broken, now fixed
      - feature: new capability or functionality added
      - refactor: code restructured, behavior unchanged
      - change: generic modification (docs, config, misc)
      - discovery: learning about existing system
      - decision: architectural/design choice with rationale
  -->
  <title>[**title**: Short title capturing the core action or topic]</title>
  <subtitle>[**subtitle**: One sentence explanation (max 24 words)]</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
  </facts>
  <!--
    **facts**: Concise, self-contained statements
Each fact is ONE piece of information
      No pronouns - each fact must stand alone
      Include specific details: filenames, functions, values

**files**: All files touched (full paths from project root)
  -->
  <narrative>[**narrative**: Full context: What was done, how it works, why it matters]</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
  </concepts>
  <!--
    **concepts**: 2-5 knowledge-type categories. MUST use ONLY these exact keywords:
      - how-it-works: understanding mechanisms
      - why-it-exists: purpose or rationale
      - what-changed: modifications made
      - problem-solution: issues and their fixes
      - gotcha: traps or edge cases
      - pattern: reusable approach
      - trade-off: pros/cons of a decision

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT include the observation type (change/discovery/decision) as a concept.
    Types and concepts are separate dimensions.
  -->
  <files_read>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```


IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this OBSERVATIONS from tool use messages - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the observation content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful observations.

Remember that we record these observations as a way of helping us stay on track with our progress, and to help us keep important decisions and changes at the forefront of our minds! :) Thank you so much for your help!

MEMORY PROCESSING CONTINUED
===========================

PROGRESS SUMMARY CHECKPOINT
===========================
Write progress notes of what was done, what was learned, and what's next. This is a checkpoint to capture progress so far. The session is ongoing - you may receive more requests and tool executions after this summary. Write "next_steps" as the current trajectory of work (what's actively being worked on or coming up next), not as post-session future work. Always write at least a minimal summary explaining current progress, even if work is still in early stages, so that users see a summary output tied to each request.

Claude's Full Response to User:
讀完了。專案**已有一條 skill 萃取管線**(`skill-suggest.ts` → bigram 頻率 → `/dream memorytoskill` → `SKILL_PROPOSE`),但它吃的是**使用者訊息**,不是 tool 使用。方案 C 的差異點就在「改用 tool 使用當訊號源」。這改變了方案 B 該怎麼調的答案。

## 先校正一個概念

方案 B(pluggable hook)**本身不會長出 skill** —— 它只是個擴充點。會長出 skill 的是方案 C 的 analyzer(observations → cluster → instinct → propose)。所以「B 要怎麼調才能達成 C 的目的」實際問的是:

> **B 的 hook 要捕捉什麼資料、寫到哪、用什麼 schema,才能讓 C 的 analyzer 直接接上?**

要調的是 **observation 的資料契約與捕捉點**,不是 hook 的 mechanism。把 B 的 hook 輸出 schema **設計成等於 C 的 `Observation` 輸入 schema**,C 就退化成「只加一個 `/dream` analyzer 步驟」。

---

## 但有個硬約束會決定整個調整方向:訊號源覆蓋

skill 萃取的品質取決於拿得到多少 tool 資料,而兩條路徑拿到的資料量天差地遠:

| 路徑 | 捕捉點 | 拿得到的資料 | 能萃取的 skill 類型 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DirectProvider** | `tool-loop.ts`(方案 B hook) | name + **args + output** + isError + 順序 | 細粒度,如「git 操作總是該帶 `--no-pager`」「grep 前先 glob」 |
| **ACP**(Kiro/Codex/Claude — **依 CLAUDE.md 是主力路徑**) | `sessionManager.ts:387/394` 的 `logEvent("tool_call",{name,status})` | **只有 name + status**,**拿不到 args/output**(agent 進程外自跑) | 只能 combo 序列,如「shell 失敗後常接 read」 |

⚠️ **這是關鍵**:方案 C 的 `Observation` schema 假設拿得到 `args` 和 `output`(proposal line 287-288)。但你日常如果跑 ACP(Kiro/Codex),tool-loop.ts 幾乎不執行,`observations.jsonl` 會接近空的 —— **C 的整個學習閉環只對 DirectProvider 模式生效**。

---

## 兩條調整路線(你得先選)

**路線 1 — 接受「只在 DirectProvider 模式學習」(忠於原 B,改動小)**
- 方案 B 加一個**唯一的 built-in observer hook**(matcher `*`),**只寫不改**(去掉原 proposal 的 mutate messages 野心)。
- hook 輸出直接寫成 C 的 `Observation` 格式到 `observations.jsonl`。
- `precedingTools` 由 `runToolLoop` 維護一個 ring buffer 餵給 hook(loop 本來就握有 turn 內順序)。
- 代價:Kiro/Codex 模式學不到東西。

**路線 2 — 路徑無關的序列學習(覆蓋主力路徑,但訊號弱)**
- **不靠 tool-loop.ts 的 hook**,改在 `sessionManager` 既有的 `tool_call` event 上補記 `precedingTools` 序列(name+status 級)。
- analyzer 只能萃取 **combo/順序型 instinct**,萃不出 args 級規則。
- 代價:萃取粒度粗,但 Kiro/Codex 都涵蓋。

**路線 3(建議)— 雙層 observer,analyzer 吃哪層有資料就用哪層**
- Tier A(sessionManager,兩路徑,name+status 序列)
- Tier B(tool-loop.ts 方案 B hook,僅 DirectProvider,含 args/output)
- C 的 analyzer 對兩層分別跑:有 args 跑細規則、只有序列跑 combo 規則。

---

## 調整後的方案 B 具體形狀(以路線 1/3 的 Tier B 為例)

相對**原提案的三個改動**:

1. **hook 從「可 mutate messages」收斂成「observation-only side effect」** —— 萃 skill 不需要改寫 message,去掉 mutate 能力就避開了我上一輪提的 `ToolResult` 擴展與 CodeGraph enrichment 語意陷阱(那些是為了「改寫」才需要的)。observer hook 只需 read。

2. **hook 的 ctx 仍要補 `name / args / isError`**(現在 `ToolResult` 只有 `{id,output}`),但因為只讀不寫,可以**直接在 executeToolCalls 內就近呼叫 observer**,連 ToolResult 介面都不用動 —— 那裡 `tc.function.name`、`args`、`result.isError`、`output` 全在手上(tool-loop.ts:210-239)。**這比原 B 更省**。

3. **新增 `precedingTools` 捕捉**:`runToolLoop` 維護 `recentToolNames: string[]`(類似已有的 `recentCalls` hash 機制 tool-loop.ts:85),餵給 observer。

4. **observer 輸出 schema 對齊 C 的 `Observation`** —— 這是讓 B 直接接上 C 的核心動作。

5. **C 退化成**:`/dream` 加一個 `instinct-evolve` 步驟,讀 `observations.jsonl`,跑 local LLM cluster(`local-llm.ts` 已有基礎設施),產 instinct → 沿用既有 `SKILL_PROPOSE` token 出口。

換句話說:**B 不再是「通用 hook registry」,而是「為 C 量身的 observation tap」**。通用 registry 的價值(零改 core 加新 hook)在「只有 observer 一個 hook」的前提下根本用不到 —— YAGNI,別建 matcher 框架,直接埋一個 observer 呼叫點即可。

---

## Push back

⚠️ **我建議反對「照原 A→B→C 漸進」的框架**

- **理由**:① 原 proposal 的 A→B→C 假設所有 tool call 流經同一處,實際是 ACP/Direct 兩路徑分裂(已用碼證實:`runToolLoop` 僅 direct.ts:121 呼叫);② 通用 hook registry 對「只有一個 observer hook」是過度設計(Q2 Simplicity);③ 細規則萃取只在 DirectProvider 成立,你若主力跑 Kiro,做完 C 會發現沒資料可學。
- **替代方案**:跳過「通用化的 B」,直接做 **「observation tap(就近埋點,不建 registry)+ C 的 analyzer」**,並先回答覆蓋路線。

**我需要你先決定一件事**才能出設計規格:

**你日常實際跑哪條路徑?**
- 主要是 **ACP(Kiro/Codex/Claude)** → 走路線 2,在 sessionManager 埋序列 observer,接受 combo 級 skill(tool-loop.ts 的 hook 對你幾乎無用)。
- 主要是 **DirectProvider(本地/直連模型)** → 走路線 1,在 tool-loop.ts 埋 observer,可萃 args 級 skill。
- **兩者都用** → 路線 3 雙層。

選定後我用 `/dev-design` 出含因果鏈分析的設計規格。要我直接問這個決策(AskUserQuestion)還是你這邊直接回?

Respond in this XML format:
<summary>
  <request>[Short title capturing the user's request AND the substance of what was discussed/done]</request>
  <investigated>[What has been explored so far? What was examined?]</investigated>
  <learned>[What have you learned about how things work?]</learned>
  <completed>[What work has been completed so far? What has shipped or changed?]</completed>
  <next_steps>[What are you actively working on or planning to work on next in this session?]</next_steps>
  <notes>[Additional insights or observations about the current progress]</notes>
</summary>

IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this next PROGRESS SUMMARY - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the summary content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful summary content.

Thank you, this summary will be very useful for keeping track of our progress!

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.thinking:
The user message "兩者都用" is in Traditional Chinese and translates to "Use both" or "Both are used". This is a very brief response that appears to be answering a question or making a choice between two options.

However, I need to understand the context. Looking at the observed_from_primary_session:
- User request: "兩者都用"
- Requested at: 2026-06-30

This is extremely minimal context. There's no information about:
- What tool was being used
- What files were being worked on
- What the two options being referred to are
- Any previous context

As a memory agent, I'm instructed to:
1. Only observe and record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED
2. Skip routine operations
3. Not do any work other than generating observations from tool use messages
4. Focus on deliverables and capabilities

Given that this is just a brief user message with no tool execution, no file changes, no deployment, no configuration changes, and no clear context about what "both" refers to, this falls under the "WHEN TO SKIP" category:
- This appears to be a simple response with no actual work performed
- No tool execution messages are provided
- No file modifications are shown
- No features were implemented, bugs fixed, or configurations changed

According to the instructions: "**No output necessary if skipping.**"

Therefore, I should not generate any observation for this, as there's nothing substantive to record for future sessions.

No observation needed at this time.

---

## 但有個硬約束會決定整個調整方向:訊號源覆蓋

skill 萃取的品質取決於拿得到多少 tool 資料,而兩條路徑拿到的資料量天差地遠:

| 路徑 | 捕捉點 | 拿得到的資料 | 能萃取的 skill 類型 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DirectProvider** | `tool-loop.ts`(方案 B hook) | name + **args + output** + isError + 順序 | 細粒度,如「git 操作總是該帶 `--no-pager`」「grep 前先 glob」 |
| **ACP**(Kiro/Codex/Claude — **依 CLAUDE.md 是主力路徑**) | `sessionManager.ts:387/394` 的 `logEvent("tool_call",{name,status})` | **只有 name + status**,**拿不到 args/output**(agent 進程外自跑) | 只能 combo 序列,如「shell 失敗後常接 read」 |

⚠️ **這是關鍵**:方案 C 的 `Observation` schema 假設拿得到 `args` 和 `output`(proposal line 287-288)。但你日常如果跑 ACP(Kiro/Codex),tool-loop.ts 幾乎不執行,`observations.jsonl` 會接近空的 —— **C 的整個學習閉環只對 DirectProvider 模式生效**。

---

## 兩條調整路線(你得先選)

**路線 1 — 接受「只在 DirectProvider 模式學習」(忠於原 B,改動小)**
- 方案 B 加一個**唯一的 built-in observer hook**(matcher `*`),**只寫不改**(去掉原 proposal 的 mutate messages 野心)。
- hook 輸出直接寫成 C 的 `Observation` 格式到 `observations.jsonl`。
- `precedingTools` 由 `runToolLoop` 維護一個 ring buffer 餵給 hook(loop 本來就握有 turn 內順序)。
- 代價:Kiro/Codex 模式學不到東西。

**路線 2 — 路徑無關的序列學習(覆蓋主力路徑,但訊號弱)**
- **不靠 tool-loop.ts 的 hook**,改在 `sessionManager` 既有的 `tool_call` event 上補記 `precedingTools` 序列(name+status 級)。
- analyzer 只能萃取 **combo/順序型 instinct**,萃不出 args 級規則。
- 代價:萃取粒度粗,但 Kiro/Codex 都涵蓋。

**路線 3(建議)— 雙層 observer,analyzer 吃哪層有資料就用哪層**
- Tier A(sessionManager,兩路徑,name+status 序列)
- Tier B(tool-loop.ts 方案 B hook,僅 DirectProvider,含 args/output)
- C 的 analyzer 對兩層分別跑:有 args 跑細規則、只有序列跑 combo 規則。

---

## 調整後的方案 B 具體形狀(以路線 1/3 的 Tier B 為例)

相對**原提案的三個改動**:

1. **hook 從「可 mutate messages」收斂成「observation-only side effect」** —— 萃 skill 不需要改寫 message,去掉 mutate 能力就避開了我上一輪提的 `ToolResult` 擴展與 CodeGraph enrichment 語意陷阱(那些是為了「改寫」才需要的)。observer hook 只需 read。

2. **hook 的 ctx 仍要補 `name / args / isError`**(現在 `ToolResult` 只有 `{id,output}`),但因為只讀不寫,可以**直接在 executeToolCalls 內就近呼叫 observer**,連 ToolResult 介面都不用動 —— 那裡 `tc.function.name`、`args`、`result.isError`、`output` 全在手上(tool-loop.ts:210-239)。**這比原 B 更省**。

3. **新增 `precedingTools` 捕捉**:`runToolLoop` 維護 `recentToolNames: string[]`(類似已有的 `recentCalls` hash 機制 tool-loop.ts:85),餵給 observer。

4. **observer 輸出 schema 對齊 C 的 `Observation`** —— 這是讓 B 直接接上 C 的核心動作。

5. **C 退化成**:`/dream` 加一個 `instinct-evolve` 步驟,讀 `observations.jsonl`,跑 local LLM cluster(`local-llm.ts` 已有基礎設施),產 instinct → 沿用既有 `SKILL_PROPOSE` token 出口。

換句話說:**B 不再是「通用 hook registry」,而是「為 C 量身的 observation tap」**。通用 registry 的價值(零改 core 加新 hook)在「只有 observer 一個 hook」的前提下根本用不到 —— YAGNI,別建 matcher 框架,直接埋一個 observer 呼叫點即可。

---

## Push back

⚠️ **我建議反對「照原 A→B→C 漸進」的框架**

- **理由**:① 原 proposal 的 A→B→C 假設所有 tool call 流經同一處,實際是 ACP/Direct 兩路徑分裂(已用碼證實:`runToolLoop` 僅 direct.ts:121 呼叫);② 通用 hook registry 對「只有一個 observer hook」是過度設計(Q2 Simplicity);③ 細規則萃取只在 DirectProvider 成立,你若主力跑 Kiro,做完 C 會發現沒資料可學。
- **替代方案**:跳過「通用化的 B」,直接做 **「observation tap(就近埋點,不建 registry)+ C 的 analyzer」**,並先回答覆蓋路線。

**我需要你先決定一件事**才能出設計規格:

**你日常實際跑哪條路徑?**
- 主要是 **ACP(Kiro/Codex/Claude)** → 走路線 2,在 sessionManager 埋序列 observer,接受 combo 級 skill(tool-loop.ts 的 hook 對你幾乎無用)。
- 主要是 **DirectProvider(本地/直連模型)** → 走路線 1,在 tool-loop.ts 埋 observer,可萃 args 級 skill。
- **兩者都用** → 路線 3 雙層。

選定後我用 `/dev-design` 出含因果鏈分析的設計規格。要我直接問這個決策(AskUserQuestion)還是你這邊直接回?

</summary>

content.thinking:
The user is asking me to generate a progress summary based on the observed session. Let me analyze what happened:

1. **User Request**: "兩者都用" (Use both) - This is the user's response to a question about which execution path they primarily use (ACP vs DirectProvider).

2. **Claude's Response**: Claude provided a detailed analysis about how to adjust proposal B to support proposal C (skill extraction from tool usage). The key points were:
   - Corrected a conceptual misunderstanding about what B does vs what C does
   - Identified a critical constraint: the data visibility differs between DirectProvider and ACP paths
   - Proposed three adjustment routes based on which execution path is used
   - Asked the user to choose which path they primarily use (ACP, DirectProvider, or both)
   - User responded "兩者都用" (both)

3. **What was investigated**: 
   - The existing skill extraction pipeline in the codebase
   - The difference between tool data captured in DirectProvider vs ACP paths
   - How proposals B and C should integrate based on the data schema

4. **What was learned**:
   - The codebase already has a skill extraction pipeline but it works on user messages, not tool usage
   - There's a fundamental difference in data availability between DirectProvider (full args/output) and ACP (name/status only)
   - Proposal C's Observation schema assumes args/output availability, which only works in DirectProvider mode
   - The original A→B→C progression has flawed assumptions about unified tool call paths

5. **What was completed**: 
   - Analysis of the existing skill extraction infrastructure
   - Identification of the hard constraint around signal source coverage
   - Design of three adjustment routes with trade-offs
   - User decision made: Route 3 (dual-layer observer for both paths)

6. **Next steps**:
   - Now that user chose "both paths", Claude will proceed with Route 3 (dual-layer observer)
   - Will use `/dev-design` to create a design specification with causal chain analysis

content.text:
```xml
<summary>
  <request>Design integration strategy for skill extraction from tool usage (proposals B→C) and choose execution path coverage</request>
  <investigated>Analyzed existing skill extraction pipeline in skill-suggest.ts, tool execution paths (DirectProvider vs ACP), data capture points in tool-loop.ts and sessionManager.ts, and the schema mismatch between proposal B's hook output and proposal C's Observation input requirements</investigated>
  <learned>The codebase has two distinct tool execution paths with drastically different data visibility: DirectProvider captures full args/output via tool-loop.ts, while ACP (Kiro/Codex/Claude) only logs name+status via sessionManager events. Proposal C's Observation schema assumes args/output availability, making fine-grained skill extraction impossible for ACP-only workflows. The original A→B→C progression assumed a unified tool call path that doesn't exist in practice.</learned>
  <completed>Identified three adjustment routes: Route 1 (DirectProvider-only with full detail), Route 2 (path-agnostic sequence learning with coarse signals), and Route 3 (dual-layer observer supporting both paths at appropriate granularity levels). User selected Route 3 to support both execution paths.</completed>
  <next_steps>Create detailed design specification using /dev-design for Route 3 dual-layer observer architecture, defining Tier A (sessionManager, name+status sequences for combo-level skills) and Tier B (tool-loop.ts, args+output for fine-grained rules), with analyzer logic that adapts to available data richness</next_steps>
  <notes>The user's choice of "both paths" (兩者都用) drives a more complex but comprehensive solution. The key insight is that proposal B should not be a generic hook registry (YAGNI for single observer use case) but rather a targeted observation tap with schema aligned to proposal C's analyzer input, allowing the skill extraction pipeline to work across both execution modes at appropriate granularity levels.</notes>
</summary>
```

content.thinking:
The user is reading the direct.ts file to see how DirectProvider calls runToolLoop and what hooks it passes. This is examining the execution path for DirectProvider, specifically looking at lines 119-174 which show:

1. The call to `runToolLoop` with callbacks: onText, onThinking, onToolStart, onToolEnd
2. onToolStart receives (name, input)
3. onToolEnd receives (name, output, isError)
4. These callbacks emit updates via emitUpdate

This is investigative work to understand where the observation hooks would need to be added for Route 3's Tier B (DirectProvider path with full args/output capture). The code shows that DirectProvider already has hook points (onToolStart, onToolEnd) that receive the necessary data (name, input/args, output, isError).

This is a "discovery" type observation - learning about the existing hook structure in DirectProvider's runToolLoop call.

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>DirectProvider's runToolLoop hook points</title>
  <subtitle>DirectProvider already passes onToolStart and onToolEnd callbacks to runToolLoop with full tool execution details</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>DirectProvider calls runToolLoop with onToolStart(name, input) and onToolEnd(name, output, isError) callbacks at direct.ts:133-140</fact>
    <fact>onToolStart receives tool name and input arguments during tool execution</fact>
    <fact>onToolEnd receives tool name, output result, and error status after tool completes</fact>
    <fact>These callbacks currently only emit SessionUpdate events, not persisted observations</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The DirectProvider execution path already has hook points for capturing detailed tool execution data. When DirectProvider calls runToolLoop, it passes four callback functions: onText for message chunks, onThinking for reasoning chunks, onToolStart for tool invocation with name and input arguments, and onToolEnd for tool completion with name, output, and error status. These callbacks currently emit real-time SessionUpdate events to the UI but do not persist observation data to disk. This existing structure provides the ideal insertion point for Tier B observation capture in the dual-layer architecture, as all necessary data (args, output, isError) is already flowing through these callbacks.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/provider/direct.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```
